issues.description: Recommendation algorithms create pathways from mainstream content to increasingly extreme material, functioning as recruitment tools for extremist movements.

reformers.name: Institute for Strategic Dialogue (ISD)
reformers.description: London-based think tank that conducts research on how extremist groups exploit social media algorithms for recruitment, mapping radicalization pathways across platforms and advising governments and tech companies on counter-extremism strategies. Their research has documented how recommendation algorithms systematically guide users toward increasingly extreme content.

reformers.name: Moonshot CVE
reformers.description: Technology company that develops counter-extremism interventions including the 'Redirect Method,' which uses targeted advertising to serve counter-narrative content to individuals searching for extremist material online. Partners with Google, governments, and NGOs to intercept radicalization at the point of algorithmic recommendation.

reformers.name: Data & Society Research Institute
reformers.description: Research organization whose reports, particularly 'Alternative Influence' (2018) by Rebecca Lewis, documented how YouTube's recommendation system created a network of increasingly radical political commentators that functioned as a pipeline from mainstream conservatism to white supremacist content.

reformers.name: Global Internet Forum to Counter Terrorism (GIFCT)
reformers.description: Industry-led organization founded by Facebook, Microsoft, Twitter, and YouTube that maintains a shared hash database of terrorist and violent extremist content to prevent re-upload across platforms. While criticized for limited transparency, it represents the most significant cross-platform coordination against extremist exploitation of recommendation systems.

reformers.name: Megan Squire
reformers.description: Computer scientist and senior fellow at the Southern Poverty Law Center who has conducted extensive research on how extremist networks use social media platforms for recruitment, mapping the technical infrastructure of online radicalization and providing data to law enforcement and journalists investigating extremist movements.

legislation.name: EU Terrorist Content Online Regulation (TCO)
legislation.description: Requires hosting service providers to remove terrorist content within one hour of receiving a removal order from a competent authority, and mandates that platforms exposed to terrorist content take proactive measures to address it, directly targeting the availability of extremist material in recommendation systems.

legislation.name: UK Online Safety Act - terrorism and extremism provisions
legislation.description: Imposes duties on platforms to proactively prevent users from encountering terrorist content and content that encourages or glorifies violence, with Ofcom empowered to require the use of proactive detection technology and to hold senior executives criminally liable for failures.

legislation.name: Christchurch Call to Action
legislation.description: Multi-stakeholder commitment by governments and tech companies to eliminate terrorist and violent extremist content online, initiated by New Zealand and France following the 2019 Christchurch mosque shootings that were livestreamed on Facebook. Not binding legislation but has driven significant policy changes.

legislation.name: Germany's revised NetzDG with extremism provisions
legislation.description: Expanded version of the Network Enforcement Act requiring platforms to not only remove but report extremist content to the Federal Criminal Police Office (BKA), including the IP addresses of posters, creating a direct law enforcement pipeline for platform-detected radicalization content.

legislation.name: Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act
legislation.description: U.S. liability shield that protects platforms from legal consequences for algorithmically recommending extremist content, meaning that even when YouTube or Facebook's recommendation systems actively guide users toward radicalization, the platforms face no civil liability for the resulting harm.

structurally_incentivized.name: YouTube (Google/Alphabet)
structurally_incentivized.description: YouTube's recommendation algorithm has been the most extensively documented radicalization pipeline, with research from multiple institutions showing it systematically guided viewers from mainstream political content to increasingly extreme material. Former YouTube engineer Guillaume Chaslot built a tool demonstrating how the algorithm preferentially recommends conspiratorial and extremist content because it maximizes watch time.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Watch-time optimization rewards provocative and extreme content that keeps viewers engaged; recommendation algorithm treats ideological escalation as successful user engagement; autoplay chains create unbroken pathways from mainstream to extreme content; the platform's scale as the second-largest search engine means its radicalization dynamics affect populations at national scale.

structurally_incentivized.name: Meta (Facebook Groups and Instagram)
structurally_incentivized.description: Facebook Groups have been documented as key infrastructure for extremist recruitment, with the platform's Group recommendation algorithm actively suggesting increasingly radical Groups to users who join ideologically adjacent communities. The algorithm's optimization for engagement meant it was, as internal researchers noted, actively recommending extremist Groups.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Group recommendation algorithm suggests progressively more extreme communities; closed and secret Group architecture provides operational security for extremist organizing; algorithmic News Feed amplifies content from extreme Groups because it generates high engagement; cross-platform ecosystem with Instagram and WhatsApp enables multi-channel radicalization.

structurally_incentivized.name: Telegram
structurally_incentivized.description: Telegram's combination of encrypted messaging, large broadcast channels, and minimal content moderation has made it the primary communication and recruitment platform for extremist movements worldwide, from ISIS and al-Qaeda to far-right accelerationist groups and QAnon communities.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Encryption and minimal moderation create a safe harbor for extremist content; broadcast channels enable one-to-many propaganda distribution; channel recommendation and search features enable discovery of extremist content; lack of cooperation with law enforcement provides impunity; forwarding mechanics enable rapid viral spread of radicalization material.

structurally_incentivized.name: Reddit
structurally_incentivized.description: Reddit's subreddit architecture created self-reinforcing extremist communities where radicalization occurred through progressive engagement with increasingly extreme content and community norms. Although the platform has since banned many extremist subreddits, the structure enabled years of radicalization before intervention.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Subreddit structure creates insular communities with self-reinforcing norms; upvote system within ideologically homogeneous communities rewards the most extreme positions; cross-posting between related subreddits creates radicalization pathways; pseudonymous accounts reduce social consequences for adopting extreme views.

reform_in_other_systems.name: EU Terrorist Content Online Regulation enforcement
reform_in_other_systems.description: The EU's TCO regulation requiring one-hour removal of terrorist content has forced platforms to develop rapid-response infrastructure for extremist material, with competent authorities across member states issuing removal orders and monitoring compliance.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Platforms operating in the EU have implemented automated detection systems and dedicated response teams to meet the one-hour removal requirement; cross-border cooperation mechanisms have been established between national authorities for coordinated enforcement.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Christchurch Call implementation
reform_in_other_systems.description: Following the 2019 Christchurch mosque attack that was livestreamed on Facebook, New Zealand and France led a multi-stakeholder initiative that brought together governments and platforms to commit to specific measures against terrorist content, including the development of the GIFCT hash-sharing database and crisis response protocols.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Led to the development of the Content Incident Protocol for rapid cross-platform response to terrorist events; expanded the GIFCT hash-sharing database; prompted platforms to invest in AI-based detection of livestreamed violence. However, the voluntary nature limits enforcement.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Germany's integrated approach to online radicalization
reform_in_other_systems.description: Germany combined platform regulation (NetzDG), law enforcement reporting requirements, and investment in counter-narrative programs and exit-assistance organizations to create a multi-layered approach to addressing algorithmic radicalization on social platforms.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Created a comprehensive model combining platform accountability with prevention and deradicalization programs; the requirement for platforms to report extremist content directly to police has generated intelligence leading to investigations and prosecutions of online radicalization networks.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Australia's eSafety Commissioner enforcement on extremist content
reform_in_other_systems.description: Australia's eSafety Commissioner has exercised powers to compel platforms to remove extremist content and to investigate systemic failures in addressing radicalization on platforms, including issuing transparency notices and fining companies for non-compliance.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Demonstrated that a dedicated online safety regulator with enforcement powers can hold platforms accountable for hosting and algorithmically promoting radicalization content; the office's transparency reports have revealed the scale of extremist material on major platforms.
